title: End-Grain Walnut & Maple Butcher Block
summary: Checkerboard end-grain block. Two glue-ups, self-healing surface, kind to your knives — and unforgiving of sloppy milling.
description:
End grain is the real thing: the wood fibers stand vertically, so the knife edge parts them instead of severing them. The board self-heals, and your knives stay sharp far longer.

It is also where beginners get humbled. You glue up a striped panel, crosscut it into slices, rotate every slice 90°, and glue up again. Every error in the first glue-up gets multiplied in the second. Milling accuracy is not optional here.

Budget for a planer or a lot of hand-flattening: end grain is brutal to surface, and a planer can tear it out badly. Light passes, sharp knives, or a drum sander if you have access to one.
timeLabel: 8–12 hrs across 3 days (two glue-ups, each needs an overnight cure)

tools.note: Non-negotiable here. You need repeatable, dead-square rips and crosscuts.
Flattening end grain by hand is possible but genuinely punishing. Take 1/64" passes and expect tearout.
At least 6. Parallel clamps really earn their price on the second glue-up.

materials.name: Hard maple, 8/4 (1-3/4" surfaced)
materials.note: ~$14/bd ft in 8/4. Thicker stock means fewer strips for the same board thickness.

materials.name: Black walnut, 8/4
materials.note: ~$18/bd ft. This is where the cost lives.

materials.name: Waterproof wood glue
materials.note: You will use twice what you expect. End grain drinks glue.

cutList.note: Each slice is 1.5" thick, cut across the striped panel. Twelve slices = the finished board's width.

steps.title: Mill everything dead square
steps.body:
Flatten, thickness, and joint all stock to a consistent 1-3/4". Every strip must be the same thickness — a 1/64" variation becomes a visible step in the checkerboard.

This step is the project. Rush it and nothing downstream will save you.

steps.title: First glue-up: the striped panel
steps.body:
Rip 1.5"-wide strips. Alternate maple and walnut — 7 maple, 6 walnut, starting and ending with maple.

Glue on edge, clamp from alternating sides, keep it flat. Light squeeze-out along every joint. Overnight cure.

steps.title: Flatten the striped panel
steps.body: Plane or sand both faces flat and parallel. Flat here means square slices in the next step. Trim one end square to give yourself a reference.

steps.title: Crosscut into slices
steps.body:
Crosscut the panel into 1.5"-thick slices — a sled with a stop block, riding the freshly squared edge.

Cut them all without moving the stop. Twelve slices at consistent thickness is what makes the checkerboard line up. Number them as they come off.

steps.title: Rotate and arrange
steps.body:
Rotate every slice 90° so the end grain faces up. Now flip every *other* slice end-for-end — that offsets the stripes by one and produces the checkerboard.

Dry fit. Nudge until the squares line up. Mark a V across the top.

steps.title: Second glue-up
steps.body:
This one is greedy. Roll a heavy, even coat on every end-grain face — end grain wicks glue away from the joint, and a starved end-grain joint is a weak one.

Clamp evenly. Watch for slices sliding out of plane; end grain is slippery under pressure. Cauls across the top and bottom help enormously. Overnight cure.

steps.title: Flatten the block — carefully
steps.body:
**Do not send end grain through a planer aggressively.** Take 1/64" passes at most, with sharp knives, and back up the trailing edge with a sacrificial block to stop blowout. A drum sander is the safer tool if you can get to one; a router sled works too.

Expect this to take a while. It is the price of end grain.

steps.title: Trim, ease, sand
steps.body:
Trim to final size — roughly 12" x 16" x 1.5". Roundover every edge.

Sand 80 → 220. Raise the grain with a damp rag between the last two grits; end grain fuzzes badly otherwise.

steps.title: Oil heavily
steps.body:
End grain is a bundle of straws standing on end and it will drink an astonishing amount of oil. Flood it, wait, wipe, repeat — expect 5–6 coats over two days before it stops absorbing.

Finish with wax paste. Then tell the recipient: hand wash only, re-oil monthly at first. This board should outlast them.
